coredata.dc:title: Quaternary ammonium-functionalized carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Kezheng
Guo, Yaqing
Niu, Qingyuan
Han, Lifeng
Zhou, Liming
Wang, Lizhen
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescence nanosensor for sensitive and selective 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) determination is reported based on (3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride (CTA) functionalized carbon dots (CTA-CDs). Carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC)-4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) mixture was heated to prepare passivated CDs. Then CTA was grafted onto the surface of the passivated CDs by esterification reaction to form quaternary ammonium-functionalized CTA-CDs. Therefore, the CTA-CDs are used as a sensor for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in water via molecular interactions (electrostatic, π−π, and hydrogen bonding interactions) between TNP and CTA-CDs. A limit of detection of the CTA-CDs for TNP in aqueous solution is 7.04 × 10−7 mol/L, which is lower than that of the passivated CDs for TNP (3.03 × 10−6 mol/L). In addition, compared with various organic reagents, the CTA-CDs exhibit significantly sensitive and selective sensing towards TNP in aqueous medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quaternary ammonium
Carbon dots
Sensitivity
Selectivity
2,4,6-Trinitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching characteristic of the CTA-CDs towards TNP in water

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM micrographs of a) the passivated CDs and b) the CTA-CDs. c) XPS survey spectra of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs. d) C1S spectra of the passivated CDs.
a) N1S spectra of the passivated CDs. b) FTIR spectra of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs. c) C1S spectra of the CTA-CDs. d) N1S spectra of the CTA-CDs.

a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs in water. b) The fluorescence spectra of the passivated CDs aqueous solution with variation excitation wavelengths. c) The fluorescence spectra of the CTA-CDs aqueous solution with variation excitation wavelengths. d) The effect of the ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs.

a) Fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs during continuous excitation at 365 nm. b) The effect of pH values on fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs. c) Fluorescence quenching characteristic of the CTA-CDs as a function of TNP concentration in water. d) Stern–Volmer plots of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs towards TNP in water.
a) Stern–Volmer plots of the CTA-CDs towards TNP and various organic reagents in water. b) Lifetime decay of the CTA-CDs before and after addition of TNP.
Schematic illustration for the sensitive and selective detection of TNP based on the CTA-CDs.
a) Comparison of the Stern-Volmer plots for TNP, DNP, and NP in water. b) Stern–Volmer plots of the CTA-CDs towards TNP at different pH values in water.

head.ce:title: Quaternary ammonium-functionalized carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Laboratory of Surface and Interface Science and Technology, School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Laboratory of Surface and Interface Science and Technology
School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CTA-CDs show high sensitivity and selectivity towards TNP in water via molecular interactions.

A new fluorescence nanosensor for sensitive and selective 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) determination is reported based on (3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride (CTA) functionalized carbon dots (CTA-CDs). Carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC)-4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) mixture was heated to prepare passivated CDs. Then CTA was grafted onto the surface of the passivated CDs by esterification reaction to form quaternary ammonium-functionalized CTA-CDs. Therefore, the CTA-CDs are used as a sensor for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in water via molecular interactions (electrostatic, π−π, and hydrogen bonding interactions) between TNP and CTA-CDs. A limit of detection of the CTA-CDs for TNP in aqueous solution is 7.04 × 10−7 mol/L, which is lower than that of the passivated CDs for TNP (3.03 × 10−6 mol/L). In addition, compared with various organic reagents, the CTA-CDs exhibit significantly sensitive and selective sensing towards TNP in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), which is one of the main nitroaromatics (NACs), is widely used in industry, especially for the manufacture of explosives, pharmaceuticals, dyes and other applications [1–3]. Due to the high water solubility, the wide use of TNP could result in serious environmental pollutant and be harmful to human and wildlife health through water and soil [4]. Thus, highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP in water is essential to monitor environmental pollution and avert terrorist threats. Up to now, fluorescent chemosensors have been explored to meet the needs of the specific detection of TNP due to high sensitivity and convenience [5]. However, several problems still exist in fluorescent sensors for TNP detection. First, the raw materials used for many fluorescent sensors are expensive, such as protein fluorophore bovine serum albumin [6], pyrenyl probes [7], and so on. Second, the preparation process of many fluorescent sensors is cumbersome, which needs multi-step synthetic routes [8–10]. Third, most fluorescent sensors are toxic and their use would pollute the environment. For example, the toxicity of heavy metal elements which have been widely existed in semiconductor quantum dots [11] and metal-organic frameworks [12] discourage their routine use. Last but not least, many fluorescent materials could only be performed in organic medium due to limited aqueous stability/solubility [13,14], which limits their real-world applications. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop a low-cost and low/non-toxic chemosensor material with a simple preparation process for highly selective and sensitive detection of TNP in water.

Photoluminescence carbon dots (CDs), which are described as a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted overwhelming research interest due to their prominent merits in regard to unique tuneable photoluminescence properties, high solubility in water, easy surface functionalization, ease fabrication, and low toxicity [15–18]. These outstanding properties of CDs could provide new opportunities to be used as sensors for chemical analysis, such as pH [16], carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotube [19], glutathione [20], temperature [21], and metal ions including Al3+ [22] and Hg2+ [23]. As for the detection of TNP in water using CDs-based sensor, there were only a few studies reported [24,25]. However, these reported CDs prepared via one-step method were not designed by specific surface modification to improve the sensitive and selective detection of TNP on the basis of their reporting original works. Therefore, exploring specific surface-functionalized design of fluorescent CDs-based sensors for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP is importantly desired.

In this work, we report for the first time the use of (3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride (CTA) functionalized CDs (CTA-CDs) as a sensor for the TNP detection in water with high sensitivity and selectivity. The passivated CDs firstly prepared by heating carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC)-4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) mixture was selected as a precursor, for the reason that carboxyl group on the surface of the passivated CDs could react with hydroxyl group, which could promptly form positive groups (quaternary ammonium and amino groups) on the surface of the CTA-CDs. Thus, non-fluorescent complex is formed by the molecular interactions (electrostatic, π−π, and hydrogen bonding interactions) between TNP and CTA-CDs, leading to highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP in water.

4,7,10-Trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA, 95%), N,N-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC, 98%), and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP, 99%) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), (3-Chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride (CTA, 60 wt% water solution), and carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC, viscosity: 800–1200mpa.s, UPS grade) were all obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grades and used without further purification.

Water-solution carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CMC using TTDDA as the passivation agent. CMC (1 g) was dissolved in distilled water (50 mL). Then TTDDA (1 mL) was added to this solution and ultrasonically agitated for 10 min. The solution was transferred into a stainless steel autoclave (100 mL volume) and heated at 200 °C for a period of 720 min. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown solution was dialyzed against distilled water through a dialysis membrane (500 Da molecular weight cut off) for 2 days to remove small molecules. Then, the dark brown solution in 500 Da dialysis membrane was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (3500 Da molecular weight cut off) for 2 days to remove unreacted CMC. The solution out 3500 Da dialysis membrane was collected and extra water was removed by rotary evaporation at 75 °C. The solid CDs dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 2 days could be used for related characterizations. Using this method, the highest mass yield of the passivated CDs can reach 83.7%. The synthetic strategy of the passivated CDs is shown in Fig. S1.

DCC (3.3 g) was dissolved in DMSO (55 mL). (3-Chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride (3 g) and DMAP (0.49 g) were dissolved in the passivated CD solution (50 mL, 1.86 g/L), to which was add DCC solution. The mixture was stirred at 100 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was finished, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove white precipitate 1,3-dicyclohexylurea (DCU). Then, the solution was dialyzed against distilled water through a membrane (500 Da molecular weight cut off) for 3 days to remove small molecules. Then, the solution in 500 Da dialysis membrane was collected and extra water was removed by rotary evaporation at 75 °C. Finally, the CTA-CDs were dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 2 days. The synthesis of the CTA-CDs is shown in Fig. S2.

The UV absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-3900H spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime analyses were performed using a FLS980-Combined Fluorescence Lifetime & Steady State Fluorescence Spectrometer (EDINBURGH INSTRUMENTS) with a 450 W xenon lamp and a Picosecond Pulsed Light Emitting Diode (EPLED-360) with an excitation wavelength 360 nm as the excitation source, respectively. The absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured by using an integrating sphere attached to an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. All the measurements were performed at room temperature. The morphology and microstructure of the samples was examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples for HRTEM were made by dropping an aqueous solution onto a copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS instrument) was conducted with Al Kα as the X-raysource to study the surface properties and structures of the samples. All binding energies were referenced to the C1s neutral carbon peak at 284.8 eV. The FT-IR spectra were measured on a Bruker TENSOR 27 with the samples prepared as KBr disk pellets. Zeta potentials of the samples were measured at a concentration of 0.5 g/L with a Zetaplus analyzer (Zetaplus, Brookhaven, USA).

The passivated CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using CMC as a carbon source and TTDDA as a surface passivation agent. For this system, commercial CMC, which is low-cost and environmental friendly, provides carboxyl groups. Besides, TTDDA is required to enhance photoluminescence intensity of the passivated CDs [18,26] and obtain CDs with amino groups [27]. Then, the CTA-CDs with quaternary ammonium groups were prepared by the esterification reaction between hydroxyl groups of CTA and carboxyl groups of the passivated CDs. The DCC/DMAP as a catalyst system is of great benefit to the esterification reaction under mild condition. Both the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs exhibit good water-solubility at room temperature.

Fig. 1 (a), (b), and Fig. S3 show the TEM images of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs. Both the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs have a spherical morphology and possess a diameter of 16–20 nm. This indicates that chemical modification of the passivated CDs neither leads to the formation of aggregates nor changes the size.

The surface composition and element analysis for the overall composition of the passivated CDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS spectrum of the passivated CDs exhibits that C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s peaks are located at 284.0, 400.0, 530.6, and 1068.7 eV (Fig. 1c), respectively. XPS results reveal that the passivated CDs mainly consist of C, N, O, and Na elements. Na elements in the passivated CDs come from CMC, while nitrogen is from TTDDA. The C1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 284.8, 286.0, 286.6, and 288.1 eV, which are ascribed to CC, CN, CO, and CO (Fig. 1d), respectively [28]. The N1s spectrum shows two peaks at 399.5 and 401.5 eV, which are attributed to the CNC and NH bands (Fig. 2 a), respectively [29]. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum of the passivated CDs (Fig. 2b) shows that the vibration bands at 1680–1558 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching and NH bending vibrations, respectively [17]. The absorption at 1054 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CN. The peak at 1392 cm−1 is from symmetric stretching carboxyl group. This peak appears in the spectra probably due to the presence of carboxyl groups in Na+ form (Na+ stemming from CMC) [30]. These results indicate that the existence of amino, carboxyl, and amide groups is on the surface of the passivated CDs.

As for the CTA-CDs, XPS results show that the CTA-CDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O elements (Fig. 1c). Compared with the passivated CDs, the intensity of Na1s peak of the CTA-CDs disappeared. The C1s spectrum of the CTA-CDs also exhibits four peaks at 284.8, 286.0, 286.6, and 288.1 eV, which are ascribed to CC, CN, CO, and CO (Fig. 2c), respectively [28]. However, curve fitting exhibits that the N1s peak of the CTA-CDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.3, 401.4 and 402.2 eV (Fig. 2d), which are attributed to the CNC, NH [31], and quaternary-ammonium-species [32], respectively. In addition, the FTIR spectrum of the CTA-CDs (Fig. 2b) exhibits that the vibration bands at 1652 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 correspond to the amide I and amide II bands, respectively, indicating the presence of the NHCO group [17]. Furthermore, Zeta potential value denotes that the negative charge is on the passivated CDs surface (−33.35 mV). However, the Zeta potential value of the CTA-CDs is 11.49 mV. The above observations allow us to conclude that the CTA-CDs with quaternary ammonium groups are successfully prepared by the esterification reaction between hydroxyl groups of the CTA and carboxyl groups of the passivated CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs in aqueous solution (0.5 g/L) are displayed in Fig. 3 a. The absorption spectra of both the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs exhibit broad absorption peaks around 260–320 nm, which are ascribed to the π−π* transition of aromatic CC bands [33,34] and the n−π* transition of the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing groups [31]. The fluorescence spectra of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs in aqueous solution (0.5 g/L) with variation excitation wavelengths are displayed in Fig. 3b and c, respectively. Similar to the other primarily reported CDs [35], both the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs show obvious excitation wavelength dependence. Upon changing the excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 420 nm, the photoluminescence peaks of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs all red-shift from 445 nm to 485 nm. As for the passivated CDs, the optimal emission occurs at 449 nm with excitation wavelength of 380 nm. However, as for the CTA-CDs, the optimal emission centers at 456 nm with excitation wavelength of 370 nm. In addition, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of the passivated CDs is measured to be 8.89%, which is much higher than that of the CTA-CDs (4.70%). It is reported that the photoluminescence properties of CDs are attributed to surface energy traps formed by an incomplete reaction [31] and different energy levels from different surface states [36]. The different optimal excitation wavelengths and the quantum yield indicate that the surface defect of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs are different. That is because the carboxyl groups on the surface of the passivated CDs could react with hydroxyl group of CTA to form quaternary ammonium on the surface of the CTA-CDs, leading to the different surface defects of the passivated CDs and CTA-CDs.

The fluorescence stability of the CTA-CDs was tested by recording the photoluminescence intensities of the CTA-CDs under different conditions, for example, changing the ionic strength, continuous irradiation under UV lamp, and controlling pH values. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensities of the CTA-CDs remain almost constant even if the concentration of NaCl solution as high as 4 mol/L, revealing that the CTA-CDs are very stable at high ionic strength. In the meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs remains unchanged after 120 min of continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the surface states of the CTA-CDs are stable after excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of the CTA-CDs exhibit almost no change on a very broad pH range from 3 to 11, but decrease in solution at lower and higher pH values (Fig. 4b). This result means that the CTA-CDs could have potential applications in a very broad range of pH values. Interestingly, the CTA-CDs solution was stored for three months at room temperature without significant changes of photoluminescence property and appearance, which still shows a homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation. Therefore, it could be concluded that the as-synthesized CTA-CDs possess high stability, which makes it profoundly suitable for the detection of TNP in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching characteristic of the CTA-CDs towards TNP in water
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that NACs (such as TNP, TNT, and DNT) are nitro-substituted electron-deficient substrates [37]. The fluorescence intensity of electron-rich fluorescence sensors could be quenched by electron-deficient NACs via electron transfer. Therefore, the electron-rich CTA-CDs can be used as a sensor for electron-deficient NACs detection. We first investigated the applicability of the CTA-CDs as a promising detecting device for TNP in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 4c, the CTA-CDs aqueous solution (0.5 g/L) in the absence of TNP exhibits a broad strong emission peak at 456 nm under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. When electron-deficient TNP is added to the CTA-CDs aqueous solution, the fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs is quenched as a function of TNP. As the concentration of TNP is 500 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs is completely quenched, suggesting highly response sensitivity of the CTA-CDs for TNP. Notably, upon the exposure to 365 nm UV light, the CTA-CDs solutions show different fluorescent colours in the presence of different concentrations of TNP (Fig. S4).

In general, the quenching behavior is theoretically described by the Stern-Volmer equation: I0 /I = 1 + KSV [Q] (1), wherein, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively; KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant; and [Q] is the concentration of quencher [38]. As shown in Fig. 4d, when the concentration of TNP is below 100 × 10−6 mol/L, the Stern–Volmer plots of the CTA-CDs are linear, indicating a static quenching. Afterward, the curve deviates from linearity and bends upward. A possible reason for the CTA-CDs to give nonlinear Stern–Volmer plots is that the PL annihilation is caused by static quenching or simultaneous dynamic and static quenching [39]. In addition, by fitting the linear curves, the KSV of the CTA-CDs towards TNP is 1.42 × 105 L/mol. The limit of detection (LOD) of the CTA-CDs towards TNP solution, which can be obtained from the equation of CL = 3S/KSV (2), (where CL is the value of LOD and S is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity for the blank samples) [40], is estimated to be 7.04 × 10−7 mol/L. For comparison, the passivated CDs aqueous solution was also used to detect TNP (Fig. 4d). The KSV of the passivated CDs towards TNP is 3.83 × 104 L/mol. The LOD of the passivated CDs towards TNP is determined to be 3.03 × 10−6 mol/L, which is 10 times higher than that of the CTA-CDs towards TNP. The result demonstrates highly response sensitivity of the CTA-CDs for TNP detection in aqueous solution.

In addition, to evaluate the sensing specificity of the CTA-CDs as a sensor for TNP, the fluorescence spectra of the CTA-CDs were performed in the presence of various organic reagents in aqueous solution including TNT, DNT, nitrobenzene (NB), phenol (PHE), benzoate, aniline, methylbenzene (MB). As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, only TNP exhibits substantial fluorescence quenching, and the KSV towards TNP is up to 1.42 × 105 L/mol. However, as for electron-deficient NACs, the KSV towards TNT, DNT, and NB are 5.08 × 102 L/mol, 2.80 × 102 L/mol, and 1.35 × 102 L/mol, respectively, indicating that TNT, DNT, and NB could quench the fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs only to a small extent. Furthermore, other reagents including PHE, benzoate, aniline, and MB are unable to lead to fluorescence quenching. Obviously, these results indicate that the CTA-CDs are high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of TNP in water, which is of great benefit to monitor environmental pollution and avert terrorist threats.

So far, there are two types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms: one is static quenching by forming non-fluorescent complexes and the other is dynamic quenching via a bimolecular collision of the excited states [41]. Dynamic and static quenching can be distinguished by fluorescence lifetimes of fluorophores as a function of quencher concentration and the effect of temperature on the extent of fluorescence quenching [42]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime measurement was carried out and the fluorescent decay curves of the CTA-CDs in the absence and presence of TNP are shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence lifetime of the CTA-CDs (7.865 ns) has almost no change after adding TNP (7.823 ns). Furthermore, the relative fluorescence intensities of the CTA-CDs at two different temperatures were plotted as a function of TNP concentration. As shown in Fig. S5, the KSV of the CTA-CDs towards TNP calculated from the linear part is 1.42 × 105 L/mol at 25 °C, which is higher than that at 45 °C (7.7 × 104 L/mol). The two results suggest that the fluorescence quenching of the CTA-CDs by TNP occurs via a static mechanism. In other words, the non-fluorescent complex is formed between CTA-CDs and TNP.

Thus, the non-fluorescent complex via the molecular interactions (electrostatic, π−π, and hydrogen bonding interactions) can explain the reason that passivated CDs shows higher LOD towards TNP. The possible reason is that the electrostatic interaction between TNP and passivated CDs without quaternary ammonium modification is weaker than that between TNP and CTA-CDs. In addition, the molecular interactions can clarify the observation that other NACs exhibit little effect upon the fluorescence intensity of the CTA-CDs. Unlike TNP, other NACs, such as TNT, DNT and NB, could not generate anion charge, and therefore they have no tendency to be enriched on the surface of the CTA-CDs.

In conclusion, quaternary ammonium-functionalized CTA-CDs were fabricated by esterification reaction between the carboxyl groups on the surface of the passivated CDs and hydroxyl groups of the CTA. The resulting CTA-CDs show good water-solubility and high fluorescence stability. Remarkably, the as-synthesized CTA-CDs can be used as a sensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP in aqueous solution. A limit of detection of the CTA-CDs for TNP is 7.04 × 10−7 mol/L, which is lower than that of the passivated CDs for TNP (3.03 × 10−6 mol/L). In addition, compared with various organic reagents, the CTA-CDs exhibit significantly selective sensing towards TNP in aqueous medium. The formation of non-fluorescent ground-state complex via the molecular interactions (electrostatic, π−π, and hydrogen bonding interactions) between TNP and CTA-CDs is proposed as a quenching mechanism. Overall, this study suggests quaternary ammonium functionalization of carbon dots is a promising approach for TNP detection in water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support was kindly supplied by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601162, No. 21501154, No. 21471135, and No. 21671178), Important Research Project at the University of Henan Province (16A430031), the Project of Henan Province Science and Technology Department (152102210352) and Doctoral Research Foundation of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry (2014BSJJ059 and 2014BSJJ060), and Foundation of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry (2015XJJZ030 and 2015XJJY004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.008.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Colloids Surf. A
Hydrothermal carbonization of carboxymethylcellulose: one-pot preparation of conductive carbon microspheres and water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanodots
Chem. Eng. J.
Graphene quantum dots sensor for the determination of graphene oxide in environmental water samples
Anal. Chem.
Carbon dots a unique fluorescent cocktail of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons
Nano Lett.
A carbon dot-hemoglobin complex-based biosensor for cholesterol detection
Green Chem.
An ionic liquid promoted microwave-hydrothermal route towards highly photoluminescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of iron(III)
RSC Adv.
Pd(0) supported on N-doped graphene quantum dot modified cellulose as an efficient catalyst for the green reduction of nitroaromatics
RSC Adv.
Water-soluble nonconjugated polymer nanoparticles with strong fluorescence emission for selective and sensitive detection of nitro-explosive picric acid in aqueous medium
ACS Appl. Mater. Interface

Langmuir
A superamplification effect in the detection of explosives by a fluorescent hyperbranched poly(silylenephenylene) with aggregation-enhanced emission characteristics

Sens. Actuators B-Chem.

Noncovalent modified graphene sheets with ruthenium(II) complexes used as electrochemiluminescent materials and photosensors
Carbon

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kezheng Gao is a professor in the School of Material and Chemical Engineering of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. He received his Ph.D. in Beijing Institute of Technology in 2014. His research concerns the detection of nitroaromatic explosives.
Yaqing Guo is a postgraduate candidate in the School of Material and Chemical Engineering of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. His research concerns chemosensors.

Qingyuan Niu is a professor in the School of Material and Chemical Engineering of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. She received her Ph.D. in Beijing Institute of Technology in 2014. Her research interest includes fluorescence sensors and their application.
Lifeng Han is a Ph.D. candidate in Zhengzhou University. His research interest includes the application of the fluorescence sensors.
Liming Zhou is a professor in the School of Material and Chemical Engineering of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. His research interest includes fluorescence sensors and their application.

Lizhen Wang is a professor in the School of Material and Chemical Engineering of Zhengzhou University of Light Industry. His research concerns the preparation and application of the fluorescence sensors.
